video_title: Extreme Valuations + Rising Volatility = 'Wild Ride' Ahead For Markets | Jonathan Wellum
gist: Jonathan Wellum of Rocklink Investment Partners argues that the US market faces a 'wild ride' ahead due to extreme valuations, AI-capex overbuilding parallels to 2000, and sovereign debt pressures, while recommending rotation into cheap insurers, commodities, and picks-and-shovels infrastructure. He endorses Trump's supply-side economic policies as directionally correct but warns the intractable global debt crisis limits their impact. The Iran conflict is seen as transitory with energy prices likely to fall, while gold and silver remain core portfolio hedges.

insights.claim: Jonathan Wellum identifies five parallel conditions between today's market and the 2000 tech bubble: explosive capex spending, sky-high valuations, market concentration among a few players, speculative FOMO fever, and uncertainty about earnings delivery and potential overbuild.
insights.speaker: Jonathan Wellum
insights.so_what: A five-for-five match with 2000 conditions is a systematic checklist for timing defensive repositioning; investors should track which conditions are deteriorating fastest.
insights.quote: explosive capex spending, sky-high valuations, market concentration amongst players, speculative fever, FOMO investing, and then the fifth is uncertainty in terms of the earnings delivery and the potential overbuild that that we're seeing the same five things

insights.claim: The NASDAQ dropped 78% from its 2000 peak of ~5,100 and took 15 years to recover that level; Wellum explicitly invokes this as a non-trivial risk scenario for current AI-driven tech valuations.
insights.speaker: Jonathan Wellum
insights.so_what: If a comparable drawdown occurs in AI-concentrated indices, an investor holding at-peak would need 15+ years to break even — underscoring the asymmetric risk of buying frothy names.
insights.quote: when things peaked in 2000, the NASDAQ hit about 5100 and then it collapsed by 78% and it took 15 years to get back to 5100 again

insights.claim: A strong US jobs report on the recording day was interpreted by markets as reducing the probability of Fed rate cuts and possibly signaling the need for hikes, triggering a sell-off in high-valuation tech stocks.
insights.speaker: Jonathan Wellum
insights.so_what: The 'good news is bad news' dynamic is alive in this market; continued labor-market resilience will keep rate-cut hopes in check and sustain pressure on high-multiple tech.

insights.claim: Wellum argues the market's perverse reaction to good jobs numbers is fundamentally wrong: private-sector growth paired with capital inflows, deregulation, and productivity gains should tame inflation over time rather than ignite it.
insights.speaker: Jonathan Wellum
insights.so_what: If Wellum is right, the Fed is operating on a stale Keynesian framework and may hold rates too high for too long, creating a policy error risk that could eventually trigger a sharp reversal.
insights.quote: if you're actually driving the private sector and growth in the private sector and shrinking the public sector and you're having a massive amount of capital coming into your country so that there's increasing productivity that does not need to lead to inflation

insights.claim: Hyperscalers are collectively proposing to spend approximately $1 trillion on AI capex in the near term; to justify a 15% return on equity, they would need to generate $150 billion in incremental profit — a figure Wellum treats as a serious arithmetic challenge.
insights.speaker: Jonathan Wellum
insights.so_what: The $1T capex / $150B required ROE math is a simple but powerful valuation stress-test; failure to deliver would trigger large-scale write-downs and equity repricing across the AI complex.
insights.quote: if you think of a trillion dollars which is what they're the hyperscalers are proposed to be spending this year next year uh in the trillion dollar range you know to gen to justify that they should be generating just say 15% return on equity that's $150 billion of profit

insights.claim: Hyperscalers have transitioned from low-capital, high-free-cash-flow businesses into high-capital-intensity businesses, fundamentally altering their operating models just as competitive pressure forces all of them to accelerate spending simultaneously.
insights.speaker: Adam Taggart
insights.so_what: The forced capex race means even the best-managed hyperscaler cannot credibly signal restraint; capital allocation decisions are being driven by competitive fear rather than NPV analysis.
insights.quote: if you are in that cohort of companies you can't afford not to play right so if Google is going you know full bore on this and you're Amazon you've got to follow suit because the prize is so big that if your competitor were to take it your business model would be existentially at risk

insights.claim: If AI fails to deliver near-term earnings growth at the scale priced in, Wellum expects the AI-pure-plays and AI-adjacent names (like Anthropic) to drop substantially, dragging the broader market down, while Google and Amazon may hold up better given their large underlying cash-flow businesses.
insights.speaker: Jonathan Wellum
insights.so_what: Diversified hyperscalers are a better risk-adjusted play than AI-pure-plays heading into a potential valuation reset; avoid names whose entire valuation rests on unproven AI revenue.
insights.quote: the anthropics and some of the AI companies and some of the other derivations if you will of the AI spend would drop a lot and I think it would take largely the whole market down for a period of time

insights.claim: Post the 2000-01 NASDAQ collapse, the best-performing sectors were energy, materials, consumer staples, utilities, and healthcare — all of which have low correlation to AI spending and are historically cheap relative to tech today.
insights.speaker: Jonathan Wellum
insights.so_what: Historical sector rotation playbook after a tech bust points to commodities and defensives; positioning in these sectors now provides both a hedge and a potential recovery trade.
insights.quote: best performing sectors were energy, materials, uh consumer staples, utilities and healthcare after that draw down

insights.claim: AI ROI must justify itself at the 'grassroots level' across the entire global economy — i.e., Main Street businesses must actually pay for and benefit from AI services — before SpaceX and hyperscaler valuations can be sustained.
insights.speaker: Jonathan Wellum
insights.so_what: Watch enterprise AI adoption rates and corporate AI budget survey data as a leading indicator of whether the capex cycle can sustain itself or will reverse.
insights.quote: AI has to justify itself at the grassroots level right across all of the industry and all of the economy of our you know the global economy and I that still is yet to be seen how all that works out

insights.claim: AI will help offset dire demographic trends — particularly collapsing fertility rates (China at 0.87 TFR, North America just above 1) — through productivity enhancement and robotics, which provides one genuine long-term demand floor for AI investment.
insights.speaker: Jonathan Wellum
insights.so_what: The demographic tailwind for AI-driven productivity is real and long-dated, making AI infrastructure a secular story even if there is a near-term valuation correction.
insights.quote: the total fertility rates in China, you know, 0.87. I mean that means you're only replacing 42% of your people in every generational turn

insights.claim: Large private companies including SpaceX, Anthropic, and OpenAI are all slated for IPOs in 2026; Wellum believes this timing is at least partly explained by insiders wanting to monetize at peak valuations before market conditions deteriorate.
insights.speaker: Jonathan Wellum
insights.so_what: The wave of mega-IPOs hitting simultaneously is a classic late-cycle signal; IPO-window timing by sophisticated insiders with superior information is itself a contrarian sell-side indicator.
insights.quote: I think there's a little bit of that. I think, well, maybe maybe a lot of that. Um, and so the markets are frothy. the excitement is there and these valuations are wonderful versus the book value

insights.claim: Seymour Schulich (founder of Franco Nevada) told Wellum he 'seldom if ever' buys IPOs because 'the seller knows more than the buyer' — a heuristic Wellum applies directly to the current mega-IPO wave.
insights.speaker: Jonathan Wellum
insights.so_what: Asymmetric information in IPOs is maximally acute for fast-changing, pre-profitability businesses like AI companies; retail investors should wait for 6–12 months of post-IPO price discovery.
insights.quote: the seller knows more than the buyer on IPOs, and I can never know enough about it

insights.claim: Ron Baron has publicly valued SpaceX at $10–20 trillion; Wellum cautions that a $20T valuation would require SpaceX to absorb a massive fraction of the global economy, representing serious execution and valuation risk.
insights.speaker: Jonathan Wellum
insights.so_what: The gap between Ron Baron's $20T target and the investable opportunity set means anyone buying SpaceX at or near IPO is implicitly funding the most optimistic possible scenario; calibrate position size accordingly.
insights.quote: for 20 trillion that has to absorb a massive amount of the economy to be able to justify that kind of valuation

insights.claim: Wellum's personal strategy for SpaceX is to wait for post-IPO price weakness rather than buy at the IPO price, citing average IPO underperformance at 6–12 months; he never recommends shorting Elon Musk but wants a better entry.
insights.speaker: Adam Taggart / Jonathan Wellum
insights.so_what: The optimal SpaceX strategy for long-term believers may be to let IPO euphoria fade, then buy into weakness — consistent with historical IPO return patterns.
insights.quote: that's my current strategy with SpaceX. I do actually want to own it, but I don't think I want to own it at the IPO price

insights.claim: Non-professionals (politicians, people entirely outside capital markets) are approaching Wellum at conferences asking how to get a piece of SpaceX — which Wellum reads as a classic froth signal.
insights.speaker: Jonathan Wellum
insights.so_what: Retail FOMO from non-investors is a late-stage bubble signal; historically this pattern precedes the final blow-off top and subsequent correction.
insights.quote: I've had people come up to me...individuals who are in politics or other completely outside of the capital markets. They say, 'Jonathan, I got to get a piece of that SpaceX.' When I hear that, I think just take a deep breath

insights.claim: Rocklink owns Amazon as a cheaper AI/cloud proxy versus SpaceX; Wellum values Amazon for its AWS business, underlying retail, and as an emerging space competitor (Blue Origin) that is not priced into the current valuation.
insights.speaker: Jonathan Wellum
insights.so_what: Amazon offers AI/cloud exposure with a built-in valuation discount relative to pure-play AI names; the optionality of Blue Origin growth is essentially free.
insights.quote: they're a cheaper version in some senses...you're not really paying for it in Amazon

insights.claim: Rocklink purchased ServiceNow after it got 'hammered' and added more on the way down; thesis is that ServiceNow will benefit from AI rather than be disrupted by it, as it helps large organizations integrate complex systems.
insights.speaker: Jonathan Wellum
insights.so_what: AI integration middleware plays are a more defensible AI bet than model developers; ServiceNow-type companies benefit regardless of which LLM model wins.

insights.claim: Rocklink owns Schneider Electric as a picks-and-shovels AI infrastructure play in the utility/data center buildout, alongside comparable Eaton; both benefit from the energy infrastructure required for data centers.
insights.speaker: Jonathan Wellum
insights.so_what: Industrial electrical infrastructure companies are a lower-risk way to participate in AI capex growth with more predictable revenue streams than the hyperscalers themselves.

insights.claim: Rocklink holds Prologis for data center real estate exposure, Brookfield infrastructure companies for general infrastructure, and recently trimmed its Rolls-Royce position.
insights.speaker: Jonathan Wellum
insights.so_what: Data center REITs and infrastructure conglomerates offer additional indirect AI exposure with more stable cash flows than technology stocks.

insights.claim: Rocklink owns Rolls-Royce for its three-part diversification: ~50% aero engine (oligopoly/duopoly), ~25% defense (benefiting from increasing global military spend), and ~25% power systems (data center energy demand), all at a reasonable industrial multiple.
insights.speaker: Jonathan Wellum
insights.so_what: Rolls-Royce is a diversified way to play AI power demand, defense spending growth, and commercial aviation recovery at a valuation that does not yet reflect AI data center exposure.
insights.quote: all three components will benefit from a lot of these technological changes that are taking place and have unique growth in each one

insights.claim: Rocklink has approximately 15% of assets in specialty insurance — specifically Markel and Kinsale — during the current soft market, buying when top-line growth is absent but combined ratios are exceptionally low (some at 60–70%), implying 30–40% underwriting margins.
insights.speaker: Jonathan Wellum
insights.so_what: Soft insurance markets with sub-75 combined ratios and growing book value are textbook value setups; when the market hardens these names should re-rate significantly and book value compounds in the interim.
insights.quote: we've got one that's running 60 70% combined ratio. So they're making 30 40% margins

insights.claim: Markel holds hidden value because two hedge funds are pressuring the company to separate its operating businesses from insurance, similar to the Berkshire Hathaway model — separation could unlock the conglomerate discount.
insights.speaker: Jonathan Wellum
insights.so_what: Activist pressure on Markel's business separation is a potential near-term catalyst on top of the underlying insurance value; worth watching for corporate action.
insights.quote: they've got a couple hedge funds that are really after them to separate them because they're not getting that value. So there's a lot of hidden value actually in Markel

insights.claim: Rocklink owns Cameco for uranium exposure, treating nuclear power as a critical energy source for AI data centers over the next 5–10 years.
insights.speaker: Jonathan Wellum
insights.so_what: Nuclear is a multi-year secular AI-power beneficiary; Cameco is the most liquid pure-play uranium equity.

insights.claim: Wellum has held gold for 16 years as a hedge against an intractable global debt crisis and currency debasement; he views gold as 'the ultimate monetary asset' for preserving purchasing power as deficits grow across all major economies.
insights.speaker: Jonathan Wellum
insights.so_what: Gold's role as a portfolio hedge strengthens as sovereign balance sheets deteriorate; Wellum treats the allocation as strategic rather than tactical.
insights.quote: there's an intractable debt crisis globally and we cannot get out of this without a lot of pain and gold is going to be a hedge in our view to that pain

insights.claim: Silver demand has exceeded production for five to six consecutive years; Wellum views this persistent structural supply-demand deficit as justification for holding silver even given 50% drawdown volatility from short-term highs.
insights.speaker: Jonathan Wellum
insights.so_what: A multi-year supply deficit in silver creates a fundamental floor; short-term volatility should be used to add exposure rather than exit.
insights.quote: the demand for silver is much larger than the production. And this has been going on for five, six years

insights.claim: Copper has a structural supply shortfall that is already present today, driven by AI/data center buildout, electrification, and solar; Wellum also highlights the irony that solar panels in space would require large quantities of silver.
insights.speaker: Jonathan Wellum
insights.so_what: Copper and silver are double-beneficiaries of AI infrastructure build (direct materials demand) and legacy industrial/energy transition demand; the supply gap makes them asymmetric long ideas.
insights.quote: if we're going to be putting solar panels in the sky, I think that's going to take a lot of silver

insights.claim: Wheaton Precious Metals recently closed a $4 billion royalty deal with BHP, signaling major royalty companies as a vehicle to fund copper mine development; Wellum cites Rick Rule's view that large royalty companies will finance the copper mining expansion.
insights.speaker: Jonathan Wellum
insights.so_what: Royalty companies are a lower-risk way to invest in the copper supply buildout needed for AI infrastructure; Wheaton/Franco Nevada-type businesses get royalty streams without taking direct mining capex risk.
insights.quote: we wheat and precious metals a $4 billion, you know, royalty deal. It was just massive with BHP

insights.claim: Rocklink owns Power Metallic (TSX: PNPN, US pink sheets: PNPNF), an unusual junior miner for them — a Quebec property showing a major copper, nickel, PGM, silver, and gold discovery backed by Eric Sprott, Robert McEwen, and other billionaire investors; it is considering a NASDAQ listing.
insights.speaker: Jonathan Wellum
insights.so_what: Power Metallic (PNPN/PNPNF) is a specific high-conviction junior mining idea with billionaire backing, potential NASDAQ uplisting as a catalyst, and multi-metal exposure in a safe jurisdiction.

insights.claim: Wellum observes that the green-energy narrative has been almost entirely displaced by AI/hyperscaler spending urgency, making it now 'vogue to be a miner and dig holes' — a sharp reversal of the prior ESG-driven capital allocation regime.
insights.speaker: Jonathan Wellum
insights.so_what: The ESG-to-AI narrative shift is driving capital back into hard-commodity mining; miners that were starved of capital during the green transition are now seeing renewed interest and capital flows.
insights.quote: we were pushing the green agenda... That's gone. They've forgotten that now...now it's vogue to be a minor and to dig holes

insights.claim: Wellum uses Doomsberg's thesis that commodity prices experience secular deflation over time due to technological efficiency gains; he expects AI to further accelerate this by improving exploration accuracy and mining productivity — but this is a 10+ year dynamic, not relevant to current 5-year investment theses.
insights.speaker: Jonathan Wellum
insights.so_what: Long-term commodity investors should size for the current supply gap trade but remain aware that AI-driven mining efficiency will eventually compress returns; the 5-year window is bullish, the 20-year window is more uncertain.
insights.quote: over time generally commodities continue to have deflation because of efficiencies

insights.claim: Wellum expects the Iran conflict to settle within approximately 6 months; he believes the US and Israel have the upper hand and are pursuing an economic strangulation strategy (cutting energy revenues) rather than infrastructure destruction to force regime change.
insights.speaker: Jonathan Wellum
insights.so_what: If correct, oil markets are pricing in a risk premium that will deflate by end-2026, creating a headwind for energy equities; Wellum explicitly recommends avoiding the energy sector unless companies can be profitable at $60–70/barrel.
insights.quote: I would say over the next 6 months or so that the US and Israel have the upper hand significantly in Iran

insights.claim: Despite the Strait of Hormuz closure curtailing major oil flows, crude prices have stayed around $90/barrel — Wellum and host both express surprise at this resilience, crediting rerouting, US production offsets, Canadian and Venezuelan supply, and energy efficiency.
insights.speaker: Jonathan Wellum
insights.so_what: Energy market resilience in the face of Hormuz disruption demonstrates greater global supply flexibility than many feared; this argues against energy being a sustained inflation driver.
insights.quote: To think that the price of energy would be just in the $90 per barrel when you've basically curtailed most of that flow in the straight of Hormuz is quite shocking

insights.claim: Wellum recommends only owning energy companies that can generate profit at $60–70/barrel, not $95, because he expects energy prices to fall substantially as the Iran conflict resolves.
insights.speaker: Jonathan Wellum
insights.so_what: Energy equity selection should be stress-tested at $60–70/barrel WTI; companies that need $90+ to be profitable carry significant downside risk on a conflict resolution scenario.
insights.quote: you can find companies that can make money at 60 and $70, not 95. because I think it's more likely to go back down substantially

insights.claim: The Hormuz disruption is permanently changing shipping and pipeline routes in ways that reduce long-term vulnerability to that chokepoint, and could accelerate uranium/nuclear adoption as a more resilient energy source.
insights.speaker: Jonathan Wellum
insights.so_what: The Iran conflict is structurally accelerating nuclear energy and pipeline infrastructure investments; uranium exposure benefits from both AI power demand and geopolitical energy security.
insights.quote: it's probably lowers risk overall in the world because it won't be as vulnerable to that strait and it's also going to I think have longer-term impacts on uranium and a nuclear

themes.theme: Macro Policy: US Debt, Trump Economics & Global Growth

insights.claim: Wellum endorses the Trump administration's economic package — tax cuts, deregulation, no-tax on tips/Social Security, 100% capital depreciation, tariff-driven reshoring — as directionally correct for growing the private sector and should produce US GDP growth of 4%+ absent the Iran conflict.
insights.speaker: Jonathan Wellum
insights.so_what: A bullish macro backdrop for US private-sector investment supports a stronger-than-expected US economy in 2026, which has both inflation and rate-path implications for bond and equity markets.
insights.quote: if that wasn't happening in Iran which again we can you know everyone can argue whether that makes sense or not...the US economy would be you know probably more like 4% plus in terms of economic growth

insights.claim: The global sovereign debt crisis is described as 'intractable' — present in the US ($40T debt plus unfunded liabilities), Canada, Western Europe, and China — and the only viable near-term strategy is growing nominal GDP faster than the deficit, not actually paying it down.
insights.speaker: Jonathan Wellum
insights.so_what: Currency debasement is the path of least resistance for all major governments; gold, real assets, and productive businesses with pricing power are the principal hedges.
insights.quote: every you know these are smart individuals and everybody knows that there's a debt crisis I mean the debt crisis is massive

insights.claim: Canada is likely already in a technical recession (two negative growth quarters), and Western Europe is described as having 'abysmal' growth, in stark contrast to the US — creating a global backdrop where the dollar and US assets face relative demand support.
insights.speaker: Jonathan Wellum
insights.so_what: Divergent growth between the US and the rest of the developed world provides fundamental support for dollar-denominated assets and complicates the bear case for US equities on a relative basis.
insights.quote: I'm sitting up here in Canada, we got no growth. I would argue it's probably negative

insights.claim: Wellum uses a '5 Ds' investment framework — Digitization, Debt, Debasement, Deglobalization, Demographics — as the five mega-trends driving portfolio construction at Rocklink.
insights.speaker: Jonathan Wellum
insights.so_what: The 5 Ds framework is a reusable screening lens: businesses that address or benefit from any of these structural forces are better positioned for the next decade than those exposed to their disruption.
insights.quote: we have what we call sort of our five Ds starting with D digitization, debt, debasement, deglobalization, and demographics

insights.claim: Wellum recommends holding extra cash earning 4%+ on US money market instruments as a hedge against volatility, enabling dollar-cost averaging into future corrections without forced selling.
insights.speaker: Jonathan Wellum
insights.so_what: At 4%+ on cash, the opportunity cost of holding dry powder is low; maintaining liquidity provides both optionality and psychological protection against panic selling in a volatile market.
insights.quote: there's nothing wrong with having a bit of a cash reserve with interest rates coming up too, especially in the United States. You can generate 4% plus on a lot of money market instruments

insights.claim: Wellum warns that if you want to speculate in high-momentum names like SpaceX, you must be 'incredibly nimble' and ready to exit quickly, because the information flow will be varied and the unknowns will create wild swings in both directions.
insights.speaker: Jonathan Wellum
insights.so_what: Short-term speculation in AI/mega-IPO names requires active risk management and stop-loss discipline; passive buy-and-hold is ill-suited for assets priced for perfection.
insights.quote: if you're going to be short-term you better be incredibly nimble because I think that there will be some wild swings
